_source: TH_EXPLORE_IT_DATA.json (authoritative structured data). Real accessions, per-item licence + open_access flag. Nothing authored by an assistant.

station.tagline: The oldest name in money is standing in the room, and he has been watching the door the entire time.

museum_objects.title: Athenian Owl Tetradrachm
museum_objects.description: A silver four-drachma coin from Athens stamped with the goddess Athena on one side and her sacred owl on the other.
museum_objects.significance: This is the coin that taught the ancient world to trust a stranger's stamp instead of weighing metal by hand — the moment money stopped being a substance and started being a promise.
museum_objects.license: British Museum Standard Terms of Use
museum_objects.plain_language: A coin so trusted 2,500 years ago that people across the whole Mediterranean accepted it without checking the silver themselves — like an ancient version of everyone agreeing a $20 bill is worth $20.
museum_objects.licence: British Museum Standard Terms of Use

resolved.via: TIER 3 — no API, hard 403. Substitute with an open-licence equivalent.

museum_objects.rights_note: CITED: the original object (rights unclear/blocked). DISPLAYED: an openly-licensed image of the same kind of object, credited to its own institution. We cite one and show the other, and we say so. | IMAGE QA FAIL: The image shows a coin from 230 B.C., while the claim refers to a coin associated with 'Alexandros — Greek Drachma', which is not directly linked to 230 B.C., and the reverse side does not depict Athena's owl as described.

museum_objects.title: Athenian Dekadrachm
museum_objects.description: An extremely rare ten-drachma silver coin, one of the largest denominations Athens ever struck.
museum_objects.significance: Big coins for big moments — this one was minted when Athens wanted the world to see its silver mines were the richest on earth.
museum_objects.license: British Museum Standard Terms of Use
museum_objects.plain_language: A jumbo coin worth ten regular drachmas, made to show off how rich and powerful Athens was.
museum_objects.licence: British Museum Standard Terms of Use

resolved.via: TIER 3 — no API, hard 403. Substitute with an open-licence equivalent.

museum_objects.rights_note: NO OPEN IMAGE FOUND — panel must show text/citation only.

museum_objects.title: Silver Tetradrachm of Athens
museum_objects.description: Classic owl tetradrachm held in the Metropolitan Museum's Greek and Roman collection.
museum_objects.significance: Every version of this coin found anywhere in the ancient world is proof of how far Athenian trust traveled on four grams of silver.
museum_objects.plain_language: The same trusted coin design, found in a museum thousands of miles from Greece, showing how far this money traveled.

display_alternatives.title: Silver Tetradrachm from Athens, 170 BC - 30 BC.jpg

museum_objects.rights_note: CITED: the original object (rights unclear/blocked). DISPLAYED: an openly-licensed image of the same kind of object, credited to its own institution. We cite one and show the other, and we say so.

image_qa.what_i_see: An ancient silver coin featuring a profile of Athena.
image_qa.why: The image depicts the obverse of a coin with Athena's head, consistent with the description of an Athenian tetradrachm.

museum_objects.title: Athenian Tetradrachm
museum_objects.description: An owl tetradrachm held at the Museum of Fine Arts, Boston.
museum_objects.significance: Another surviving witness to Athens' silver empire, quietly sitting in a case thousands of years after the mines went cold.
museum_objects.plain_language: One more surviving example of the coin that made Athens the financial capital of the ancient world.

resolved.via: long tail — needs a per-domain licence rule

museum_objects.rights_note: CITED: the original object (rights unclear/blocked). DISPLAYED: an openly-licensed image of the same kind of object, credited to its own institution. museum_objects.title: Silver Ten-Drachm of Athens
museum_objects.description: A dekadrachm held at the Numismatic Museum in Athens, in the very city that struck it.
museum_objects.significance: This coin never left home — it's the rare case where the money and its origin story sit in the same room, 2,500 years later.
museum_objects.license: Numismatic Museum Athens Terms of Use
museum_objects.plain_language: A giant silver coin still kept in the same city where it was made almost 2,500 years ago.
museum_objects.licence: Numismatic Museum Athens Terms of Use

resolved.via: long tail — needs a per-domain licence rule

museum_objects.title: Lydian Electrum Stater
museum_objects.description: One of the earliest coins ever struck, made from a natural gold-silver alloy in the kingdom of Lydia.
museum_objects.significance: Before Alexandros had a face or a name, this rough lump of stamped metal was the very first draft of the idea of money.
museum_objects.license: British Museum Standard Terms of Use
museum_objects.plain_language: Possibly the very first coin ever made — before this, nobody had ever stamped a piece of metal to guarantee its value.
museum_objects.licence: British Museum Standard Terms of Use

resolved.via: TIER 3 — no API, hard 403. Substitute with an open-licence equivalent.

primary_sources.title: The Athenian Agora, Vol. XXVI: The Greek Coins
primary_sources.author: John H. Kroll
primary_sources.description: Definitive scholarly catalogue of coins excavated from the Athenian Agora.
primary_sources.held_at: American School of Classical Studies at Athens
primary_sources.plain_language: A giant academic book that catalogues every ancient coin ever dug up in the middle of Athens.
primary_sources.institution: American School of Classical Studies at Athens

resolved.via: long tail — needs a per-domain licence rule

primary_sources.title: Greek Coins
primary_sources.author: Colin Kraay & Max Hirmer
primary_sources.description: Landmark photographic and scholarly survey of ancient Greek coinage.
primary_sources.plain_language: A classic photo book showing what ancient Greek coins actually looked like.

primary_sources.title: Athens's oldest coins reveal ancient, widespread silver trade
primary_sources.author: Science.org
primary_sources.description: Scientific analysis of trace elements in early Athenian coins tracing the silver's origin.
primary_sources.plain_language: Scientists tested old coins to figure out exactly which mines the silver came from — like a DNA test for money.

resolved.via: TIER 3 — no API, hard 403. Substitute with an open-licence equivalent.

primary_sources.title: Thorikos Project Archive
primary_sources.author: Ghent University
primary_sources.description: Archaeological research archive on the Laurion silver-mining district that funded Athenian coinage.
primary_sources.plain_language: Records from the actual ancient silver mines that paid for Athens' coins.

resolved.via: long tail — needs a per-domain licence rule

primary_sources.title: Mapping Ancient Athens
primary_sources.author: Dipylon Society
primary_sources.description: GIS-based digital mapping project reconstructing the topography of ancient Athens, including the mint and agora.
primary_sources.plain_language: A digital map that recreates ancient Athens street by street, showing exactly where the money was made and spent.

resolved.via: long tail — needs a per-domain licence rule

world_map.map_description: Alexandros' silver drachma began in the mines south of Athens and traveled along trade routes reaching from Egypt to Central Asia, becoming the first truly international currency.

nodes.label: Athens Mint
nodes.description: Where Athenian silver was struck into the owl tetradrachms that became the ancient world's reserve currency.

nodes.label: Laurion Silver Mines
nodes.description: The mines that supplied the silver behind Athens' entire monetary and naval power.

nodes.label: Cape Sounion
nodes.description: A key point along the coast where silver shipments and trading vessels passed on their way to and from Athens.

nodes.label: Alexandria, Egypt
nodes.description: Greek coinage circulated widely here after Alexander the Great's conquests spread Greek currency across the Mediterranean.

nodes.label: Persepolis, Persia
nodes.description: Greek coins reached deep into former Persian territory, evidence of how far this currency's trust extended.

nodes.label: Bactria (Central Asia)
nodes.description: The furthest edge of the Greek monetary world, where owl-stamped coins still turn up in hoards today.
